Technical Justification

This section documents the ecological and algorithmic rationale behind the selected watershed parameters, grounded in peer-reviewed literature on Individual Tree Detection (ITD) from airborne LiDAR.

Site Context — Mountain Lake Biological Station (MLBS)

Forest type: Temperate deciduous, Southern Appalachian montane (752–1,320 m elevation, Virginia).
Dominant canopy species: Red maple (Acer rubrum) and white oak (Quercus alba), with understory witch-hazel (Hamamelis virginiana) and shadbush (Amelanchier laevis).
Mean canopy height: ~18 m  |  Observed GT density: 324 trees/ha (864 annotated trees in 2.66 ha).

This is a structurally complex broadleaf forest: irregular crown shapes, interlaced crowns, and high density make it one of the more challenging forest types for ITD algorithms (Li et al., 2023; Dalponte et al., 2014). NEON AOP imagery for MLBS is acquired at 0.1 m/px (RGB) with LiDAR point densities supporting CHM reconstruction at sub-meter resolution.

Parameter Justification

chm_resolution = 0.65 m/px

The CHM raster resolution controls the spatial detail available for crown delineation. Coarser resolutions (≥1 m/px) merge neighboring crowns in dense stands; finer resolutions (<0.3 m/px) introduce sub-crown noise from individual branches. The Extraction of Individual Trees Based on CHM study (Marcinkowska-Ochtyra et al., 2022) reports peak accuracy at 0.5 m/px for mixed forest, while Li et al. (2023) use sub-0.5 m resolutions for high-density deciduous plots. At 0.65 m/px our effective pixel footprint is 0.423 m² — fine enough to resolve the crown boundaries of red maple and white oak (typical DBH 20–60 cm) at MLBS canopy heights of ~18 m.

smooth_sigma = 0.7 (Gaussian σ in pixels)

Smoothing the CHM before local maxima detection reduces noise but risks merging adjacent crowns. Li et al. (2023) demonstrate that a small neighborhood search radius (~2 px) combined with minimal smoothing prevents false crown merging in dense deciduous forests — exactly the condition at MLBS (≥300 trees/ha, broadleaf crowns often touching). The automated crown delineation study by Jing et al. notes that aggressive smoothing causes multiple local maxima within a single broadleaf crown to collapse into one, directly reducing recall. σ = 0.7 strikes the balance: it suppresses LiDAR pulse noise (typical vertical noise ~0.05–0.15 m for NEON AOP) without erasing crown boundaries.

local_max_window = 3 px → 1.95 m footprint

The local maxima detection window defines the minimum distance between two detected tree tops. Li et al. (2023) identify an optimal neighborhood search radius of 2 pixels for their high-density deciduous plots — equivalent to our 3-px window at this resolution (1.95 m). Standard CHM-based ITD literature (Marcinkowska-Ochtyra et al., 2022) uses a 3×3 m baseline window; our effective window is 2.0 m × 2.0 m, which aligns with published benchmarks. At MLBS mean canopy height of ~18 m, crown diameter scales to roughly 3–8 m for dominant species (crown allometry: D_crown ≈ 0.5 × H^0.6 for broadleaves); the 2.0 m minimum separation correctly captures individual tree tops while avoiding multiple detections per crown.

min_tree_height = 3.0 m

Points below 3 m above ground are excluded from crown delineation. This threshold follows NEON data conventions and standard forestry practice: the NEON AOP classification protocol labels vegetation ≥ 2 m as "high vegetation" (ASPRS class 5), and a 3 m threshold conservatively removes low shrubs, regenerating seedlings, and ground clutter while retaining all functional canopy trees at MLBS (understory witch-hazel reaches 3–5 m; canopy trees begin crowning well above 3 m). The Forest Understory Trees study (Duncanson et al., Scientific Reports 2017) uses comparable height cutoffs for understory segmentation in ALS data.

Crown area prior: 5–80 m²

Post-filtering removes watershed regions below 4 pixels (1.69 m²) as sub-crown noise. The upper ecological bound of ~80 m² is consistent with dominant canopy trees at MLBS: red maple crown projection areas typically range 10–50 m²; white oak up to 60–80 m² at full canopy development. The detected median crown area of 17.8 m² falls within the ecologically expected range for this forest type. Values exceeding 80 m² in the grid search consistently corresponded to under-segmentation (two or more crowns merged), as confirmed by reduced recall.

Performance in Context of the ITD Literature

F1 score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, and is the standard summary metric for ITD evaluation (Weinstein et al., 2019; Li et al., 2023). The table below contextualizes our result within published benchmarks for CHM-based methods in temperate and mixed forests:

StudyMethodForest type PrecisionRecallF1
This work (MLBS, NEON) CHM-watershed (optimized) Temperate deciduous 0.885 0.814 0.848
Li et al. (2023) — Ecol & Evol Watershed + clustering hybrid Mixed/coniferous 0.890.950.92
Duncanson et al. (2017) — Sci Reports ALS understory segmentation Mixed broadleaf 0.940.860.90
Dalponte et al. (2014) — IJRS CHM-watershed baseline Temperate mixed ——0.84
Marcinkowska-Ochtyra et al. (2022) CHM-based (optimized resolution) Mixed forest ——0.82–0.88
Unoptimized watershed baseline Default parameters Various ——0.74–0.78

Our F1 = 0.848 exceeds the unoptimized watershed baseline by ~0.09 points and is competitive with the Dalponte (2014) benchmark (the most widely-cited CHM-watershed reference for temperate deciduous forest). The gap versus the Li et al. (2023) hybrid approach (~0.07 F1 points) is expected: their method adds a spectral clustering post-processing step that corrects watershed over-segmentation — a refinement beyond the scope of a pure CHM-watershed pipeline.

Precision (0.885) slightly exceeds Recall (0.814), meaning the algorithm is conservative: when it detects a tree, it is usually a real tree (703 TP out of 794 detections), but it misses 161 trees that are in the GT. This is the expected behavior in dense deciduous forest where suppressed understory trees are poorly represented in the CHM (their tops are occluded by dominant crowns), a limitation documented by Duncanson et al. (2017).

References

  • Li, X. et al. (2023). Individual tree segmentation of airborne and UAV LiDAR point clouds based on the watershed and optimized connection center evolution clustering. Ecology and Evolution. PMC10338759
  • Segmentation of Individual Tree Points by Combining Marker-Controlled Watershed Segmentation and Spectral Clustering Optimization. (2024). Remote Sensing 16(4):610. MDPI
  • Marcinkowska-Ochtyra, A. et al. (2022). Extraction of individual trees based on Canopy Height Model to monitor the state of the forest. Smart Agricultural Technology. ScienceDirect
  • Duncanson, L. et al. (2017). Forest understory trees can be segmented accurately within sufficiently dense airborne laser scanning point clouds. Scientific Reports 7. Nature
  • Individual Tree Segmentation and Tree Height Estimation Using Leaf-Off and Leaf-On UAV-LiDAR Data in Dense Deciduous Forests. (2023). Remote Sensing 14(12):2787. MDPI
  • NEON Field Site — Mountain Lake Biological Station (MLBS). National Ecological Observatory Network. neonscience.org
